The width of the external reinforcement, bf, was 100 mm in all cases, while the distance of the end of laminate from the support, a, was 300 mm for beam type A and 400 for beams type B ( Fig. 2). Moreover the reinforcement was always applied symmetrically with respect to the mid-span. An inductive transducer was positioned on the bottom side of the beam at the mid-span to measure the deﬂection during each test. Several strain gauges were glued on the CFRP sheets to analyze the stress transfer at the interface according to the typical distribution drawn in Fig. 3 . In Table 1 the following parameters are reported for the beams tested monotonically: – a = distance of the end of FRP laminate/bar from the support, – L s = shear span of the beam: Ls = L/2 /C0 120 mm, – Af = area of the FRP, – nf = number of layers/bars of FRP glued on the bottom of beam, – steel percentage in tension: qs ¼ As d /C1 b ; being As the area of steel in tension, – steel percentage in compression: q0 s ¼ A0 s d /C1 b ; being A0 s the area of steel in tension – equivalent reinforcement percentage: qeq ¼ qs þ Ef Es /C1 Af b /C1 H ; deﬁned including both steel in tension and FRP areas, Ef and Es being the Young’s modulus of steel and ﬁbres, b and d the width and the effective depth of the concrete transversal section ( Fig. 1 ). Beams A7 and B7 had an U-shaped wrapping of the section at the ends ( Fig. 4a), while beams A5 and B5 had the same U-shaped strips distributed along the whole length (Fig. 4b). On the contrary, beams strengthened with NSM carbon bars had no end devices. Beams A9 and A10, strengthened with two carbon bars, have the same equivalent reinforcement percentage of beam A3, strengthened with two layers of carbon ﬁbres. Similarly, beam B8 shows the same equivalent reinforcement percentage of beam B3. The same data of Table 1 are reported in Table 2 for the ﬁve beams tested cycli- cally; for the beams subjected to actions with load sign inversion (A6, B6, B6b) the geometrical parameters are related to the both strengthened sides (bottom and top). For all beams tested cyclically no anchorage system was included. 2.2. Materials The mean value of cubic compressive strength of concrete was 33.6 MPa, as fur- nished by experimental tests on three cubic specimens side 150 mm. Mechanical properties of steel bars have been evaluated by experimental tensile tests too; in Table 3 the mean values of yielding, fy,s, and ultimate, fu,s, tensile stress with the relative standard deviation are summarized for each bar diameter (8, 10, 12 mm). Experimental tensile tests (ASTM 3039 [30]) on the round carbon bars used as NSM reinforcement conﬁrmed a linear elastic behaviour until failure: in Table 3 the mean values of tensile strength, fu,f, and Young’s modulus, Ef, are reported with the corresponding standard deviation. The bars present a ribbed surface ( Fig. 1 b). 100 12 3 47 56 8 9 1 0 11 12 13 402 500 309 216 37 80 123 Fig. 3. Position of strain gauges along the external reinforcement. loading. This change in slope probably corresponds to the occur- rence of buckling phenomena in the compressed ﬁbres. 5. Comparison with theoretical formulations at ultimate loads Several formulations to predict the maximum stress in the external FRP reinforcement to avoid end debonding phenomena have been proposed (e.g. Fib Bulletin [2]; CNR DT200 [5]; Chen and Teng [32]; Oehlers et al. [33]; Teng and Yao [34]); most of them have a similar approach and are very reliable due to the huge amount of experimental tests used to calibrate them. About the intermediate debonding, that usually starts in corre- spondence of a shear or ﬂexural crack intersecting the reinforce- ment and then propagates horizontally along the plate–concrete interface, the simplest approaches provide a maximum strain in the FRP reinforcement to be used in the ultimate state analysis of the section (CNR DT200 [5]; Teng et al. [10]). This strain is usually greater than the value suggested for the end debonding. More com- plex approaches are based on the stress variation in the FRP rein- forcement between cracks or on the limitation of shear stresses at the FRP–concrete interface (Oller et al. [35]; Matthys [13]). On the contrary, reliable proposals to predict the failure load in RC elements strengthened with NSM systems are still few (Seracin- o et al. [7]; De Lorenzis and Teng [19]). For the end debonding the formulations suggested by Fib Bulle- tin [2], CNR DT200 [5], Chen and Teng [32] have been considered; these approaches assume that in FRP externally strengthened ele- ments the behaviour at the end of the FRP reinforcement can be assimilated to what happens in bond tests, if the shear stresses are predominant and the normal ones are negligible. However, it has to be underlined that the probabilistic meaning of these provi- sions is not always the same; about this topic a correct probabilis- tic approach, based on design by testing procedures (Monti et al. [36]), should provide mean, characteristic and design formulations. In the following the formulations to calculate the maximum tensile load, N max, at the end of the FRP reinforcement and the effective length, Le, suggested by the three aforementioned models are summarized. (1) Fib Bulletin 14 [2] Nmax ¼ a /C1 c1 /C1 kc /C1 kb /C1 bf /C1 ﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃ Ef /C1 tf /C1 fctm q kb ¼ 1:06 ﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃ 2 /C0 bf =b 1 þ bf =400 s Le ¼ ﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃ ﬃ Ef /C1 tf 2 /C1 fctm s ð1Þ where a = 0.9, c1 = 0.64, kc = 0.67, c2 =2 ; (2) Chen and Teng [32] Nmax ¼ a /C1 bp /C1 bf /C1 Le /C1 ﬃﬃﬃ ﬃ f 0 c q bp ¼ ﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃ 2 /C0 bf =b 1 þ bf =b s Le ¼ ﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃ ﬃ Ef /C1 tfﬃﬃﬃ ﬃ f 0 c p s ð2Þ where a is a coefﬁcient experimentally calibrated equal to 0.427 for mean values or 0.315 for characteristic (95% exceedance) ones; (3) CNR DT200 [5] Nmax ¼ bf /C1 ﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃ 2 /C1 kG /C1 Ef /C1 tf /C1 kb /C1 ﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃ fck /C1 fctm pq kb ¼ ﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃ 2 /C0 bf =b 1 þ bf =400 s Le ¼ ﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃ ﬃ Ef /C1 tf 2 /C1 fctm s ð3Þ where kG is a coefﬁcient experimentally calibrated equal to 0.064 or 0.030 for mean and characteristic (95% exceedance) provisions, respectively. In the previous formulations fctm is the mean tensile strength of concrete, while fck and f 0 c are the characteristic and mean values of the cylinder compressive strength of concrete, respectively; more- over b f, tf, and Ef are width, thickness and Young’s modulus of the FRP reinforcement,

Experimental performances of RC beams strengthened with FRP materials F. Ceroni * Engineering Dept., University of Sannio, Benevento, Italy article info Article history: Received 14 April 2008 Received in revised form 14 March 2010 Accepted 23 March 2010 Available online 10 April 2010 Keywords: RC beams CFRP externally bonded reinforcement NSM FRP bar reinforcement Cyclic loads Debonding Anchoring devices abstract The present paper illustrates the results of an experimental program on Reinforced Concrete (RC) beams externally strengthened with carbon Fibre Reinforced Plastic (FRP) laminates and Near Surface Mounted (NSM) bars under monotonic and cyclic loads, the latter ones characterized by a low number of cycles in the elastic and post-elastic range. Comparisons between experimental and theoretical failure loads are discussed in detail. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Strengthening of Reinforced Concrete (RC) elements by external bonded Fiber Reinforced Plastic (FRP) materials is one of the most widely adopted solutions for retroﬁtting existing structures; most applications are related to shear and ﬂexural reinforcement, con- ﬁnement of columns and joints. National and international code provisions for the design of elements strengthened with FRP Exter- nally Bonded Reinforcements (EBR) are also being issued world- wide (ACI Committee 440.2R [1]; Fib Bulletin 14 [2]; ISIS Canada [3]; Japan Society of Civil Engineers [4]; CNR DT200 [5]). RC elements generally fail because of the crushing of com- pressed concrete and/or yielding of the internal steel reinforce- ment. FRP materials show a linear elastic behaviour up to failure, but in general their high strength is not achieved when they are used as external reinforcement, because the strengthened ele- ments often fail due to a mechanism that is known as debonding. This failure mode depends on the bond behaviour at the con- crete–FRP reinforcement interface and generally happens with the detachment of a more or less thick concrete cover. Location of failure along the beam and thickness of concrete detached de- pend mainly on cracking pattern, internal steel reinforcement per- centage, presence of steel stirrups, loading scheme, and interaction between shear and normal bond stresses along the interfaces (Yao and Teng [6]; Seracino et al. [7]). As a result, the ductility of EBR strengthened RC members depends chieﬂy on failure modes which will occur. End anchorage systems could be very useful to avoid or delay the debonding failure and to achieve relevant increasing in strength and ductility (Ceroni et al. [8]). Many experiments have been carried out to assess the ﬂexural response of RC members externally strengthened with FRP lami- nates and sheets (e.g. Fanning and Kelly [9]; Teng et al. [10]; Ceroni et al. [11]; Ceroni and Pecce [12]; Matthys [13]; Yao and Teng [6]; Gao et al. [14]; Kotynia et al. [15], among others) and were aimed to investigate the performances both at serviceability and ultimate limit state. A recent and increasingly widespread strengthening technique consists in using Near Surface Mounted (NSM) FRP bars or strips inserted in grooves cut in the concrete surface on the tension side (Hassan and Rizkalla [16]; El-Hacha and Rizkalla [17]; Barros and Fortes [18]; De Lorenzis and Teng [19]; Teng et al. [20]). This strat- egy of retroﬁtting is very attractive due the simple application pro- cedures and the possibility to mitigate the risk of pre

and Barros [21]; De Lorenzis and Teng [19]; Seracino et al. [7]; Bianco et al. [22]). Moreover most of the experimental tests carried out under cyc- lic loads have been focussed on studying fatigue phenomena; thus they are characterized by a high number of cycles mainly within the elastic range (e.g. Shahawy and Beitelman [23]; El-Tawil et al. [24]; Kim and Heffernan [25]; Tan [26]; Aidoo et al. [27]; Hef- fernan and Erki [28]). On the contrary, if a low number of cycles oc- curs in the post-elastic ﬁeld, the seismic response of the structure is involved and the inelastic performances of strengthened ele- ments could be investigated. Design indications about the effect of cyclic loads on bond behaviour are still lack also due to difﬁculty to deﬁne the test set-up especially when reversed loads are considered. Bond tests under cyclic actions without reversing the load (Nigro et al. [29]) showed that the debonding force seems to be not affected by cyclic loading and a small degradation of the shear stress at the interface was detected; the cyclic degradation was still more evident for laminates than for sheets. Furthermore buckling phenomena of ﬁ- bres when they are stressed in compression have been observed in some cases (Tan [26]). This work discusses the results of an experimental program of RC beams equipped of external strengthening made of carbon FRP sheets or NSM FRP carbon bars. Monotonic and cyclic loading histories have been applied according to a four-point test scheme. Moreover end or distributed U-shaped anchoring devices have been applied when the strengthening is made of FRP carbon sheets. 2. The experimental program 2.1. Geometry of specimens and test set-up The experimental program is made of ﬂexural tests carried out on 21 RC beams; the beams are divided in two groups, labelled series A and B, respectively. All spec- imens were 100 mm in width, 180 mm deep and employed two bars of diameter 8 mm as internal steel reinforcement in compression. Series A and B ( Fig. 1a) differ in the internal tensile steel reinforcement and the span length: two 10 mm diame- ter bars with a span length L = 2.0 m for series A and two 12 mm diameter bars with a span length L = 1.8 m for series B. For all beams 8 mm diameter steel stirrups were placed along the entire length of the beam with a spacing of 150 mm. Values of infe- rior and superior cover were 30 and 50 mm, respectively, that furnish effective depths, d, of 150 mm and 130 mm ( Fig. 1 a). One beam of each series (A1, B1) was tested without any FRP strengthening in order to be used as a benchmark; moreover 16 beams were strengthened with FRP carbon sheets (EBR technique) and three beams with two FRP carbon bars (8 mm diameter) placed in two grooves realized on the bottom side of the beam (NSM technique) and then ﬁlled with an epoxy paste. Grooves were realized with a square section side 15 mm ( Fig. 1 b) that corresponds to an epoxy cover thickness-to-bar diameter ratio of about 1.9; this value is greater than the minimum one 1.5 sug- gested to avoid the splitting failure of the epoxy ﬁller surrounding the bars (De Lorenzis and Teng [19]). Four-point bending tests were carried out for all beams; tests were performed under loading control and the load was applied by a mechanical device in two points located at 120 mm on either side of the mid-span ( Fig. 2 ). All beams exter- nally strengthened were pre-loaded until 9 kN and then un-loaded to obtain a cracking patter before applying t

Finally, the wet lay-up system used as external reinforcement is made of unidi- rectional carbon ﬁbre having a texture with a density of 300 g/m 2 that corresponds to have a thickness tf = 0.167 mm; the mechanical properties given by manufacturer are reported in Table 3 . 3. Experimental results of monotonic tests 3.1. Failure mode and loads Table 4 summarizes the outcomes of the monotonic tests: the maximum load, Fmax, the increase of strength due to the external reinforcement, DFexp, with regard to the benchmark specimen, the measured deﬂection, dmax, corresponding to Fmax, the tensile stress in the reinforcement at the ultimate load, rFRP, and the fail- ure mode. The values of rFRP are calculated by multiplying the experimental strain measured at mid-span by the Young’s modulus of the FRP reinforcement (Table 3 ). Reference beams (A1, B1) failed for crushing of concrete in com- pression with steel yielded. Beams strengthened with FRP sheet and without anchoring devices failed for debonding started at the end ( Fig. 5a–b ), except beam A2 where the debonding hap- pened at the mid-span (intermediate debonding, Teng et al. [10]) contemporaneously to the crushing of concrete in compression. The increased strength was very signiﬁcant for type A beams characterized by the lower internal steel percentage and varies be- tween 26% and 51% depending on the various conﬁgurations of external reinforcement. In particular the maximum strength was attained in beam A5 having distributed carbon U-shaped strips that prevented the occurrence of debonding and led to a tensile failure of the longitudinal reinforcement and to the crushing of concrete in compression (Fig. 5 c). Also for beam A7, having anchor- ing devices only at the end, a large increase in strength was ob- tained (+39%), however, even if the end debonding took occurred after the rupture of the U-shaped strip ( Fig. 5 d). Beam A8 (Fig. 5 b) clearly showed a maximum load lower than beam A2 of about 26% due to the inferior amount of internal steel reinforcement. In beams type B the ultimate load increase compared to the ref- erence specimen resulted less (18–30%) due to the greater amount of internal steel reinforcement, as already observed in other exper- imental tests on similar RC beams (Ceroni et al. [11]). Moreover all beams without anchoring devices debonded at the end, probably due to the greater distance of the FRP reinforcement from the sup- port compared to the beams of series A (400 mm vs. 300 mm), among which at least in one case (A2) the intermediate debonded has occurred. Analogously the beneﬁcial effect of a double FRP layer com- pared to only one was observed in the beams type A (+35% vs. +26%) and is completely absent in type B. Even among those type B the beams with anchoring devices showed the highest strength increase (+25–30%), but the end and the distributed U-shaped devices produced similar effects. In pres- ence of anchoring strips only at the end of the FRP reinforcement (beam B7), the tensile fracture of ﬁbres and the subsequent deb- onding has happened at the end (Fig. 5 e), while for the beam with distributed U-shaped devices (B5) the tensile failure of ﬁbres and crushing of concrete in compression has occurred at the mid-span. The experimental values of tensile stresses show that the high strength of the FRP reinforcement was not fully utilized due to the crushing of concrete or to debonding. Moreover even for the beams where the failure of ﬁbre in tension has r

3.2. Load–deﬂection behaviour In Fig. 6a for series A and in Fig. 6b for series B the load–deﬂec- tion curves, F– d, of all beams monotonically tested and strength- ened with EBR systems are plotted together with the benchmark specimen. Both values of strength and maximum displacement are limited in most cases by debonding. The best performance among series A is shown by the beam A5 (with distributed U-shaped strips), that, as already discussed, thanks to the tensile failure of ﬁbres and the crushing of concrete in compression was able to attain a failure more ductile than the other strengthened beams. Moreover, the increasing of stiffness was clearly relevant after the yielding of steel bars, that was shifted to higher loads because of the external FRP reinforcement. The F– d curves of the beams strengthened with one layer (A2, A5, A7) show the same slope, that becomes lightly higher for the beam A3 strengthened with two layers. Similar performances were observed for the beams type B (Fig. 6 b) although, as the steel reinforcement percentage grows, the increasing of stiffness and strength due to the external rein- forcement is less substantial. Furthermore, differently from the beams type A, also when the debonding occurred, the behaviour at failure was more ductile as evidenced by the trend of the F– d curves that, after the detachment of the FRP reinforcement, overlap to the curve of the unstrengthened beam B1. Moreover the F– d curves of the beams A5 and B5, failed due to the crushing of compressed concrete and to the tension of ﬁbres at mid-span, among the strengthened beams attained the higher deﬂection in correspondence of the maximum load. In Fig. 6c and d the load–deﬂection behaviour of the beams strengthened with NSM carbon bars is compared with both the benchmark (A1, B1) and the EBR strengthened beams (A3, B3) hav- ing similar equivalent reinforcement percentages. The beam B8 exhibited a very ductile failure (Fig. 6 d) due to the crushing of con- crete that has occurred before the peeling of the cover, despite the maximum load and the effectiveness of the strengthening was higher than the equivalent beam B3 (+45% vs. 16%, that means three times larger); moreover no signiﬁcant difference in stiffness was observed between them. When the steel percentage is lower (series A, Fig. 6c), the effec- tiveness of the NSM strengthening grows further, but the increas- ing of effectiveness respect to the equivalent EBR system (i.e. beam A3) becomes lower (+55 and +72% vs. +35%, that means about two times larger). Finally, the beam A10 shows, before the steel yielding, a stiff- ness lower than the reference beam A1, probably due to a not suit- able application procedure of the epoxy paste in the grooves that inﬂuenced the effectiveness of the bond behaviour at the bar- epoxy-concrete interfaces. The distribution of strains along the external strengthening can give useful information about the bond at the FRP–concrete inter- face and the inﬂuence of cracking phenomena. In Fig. 7a the distri- bution of strains for the beams B2 and B3 at three load levels (about 30%, 60% and 90% of the maximum load) shows that, only after steel yielding (i.e. at 45 kN), in presence of two layers of ﬁbres (beam B3) the measured strains become sensibly lower than in the case of one layer (beam B2). Moreover at the ﬁrst two examined load levels the increasing of strains is proportional to the load, Fig. 5. Failure modes of beams externally strengthened with FRP

strengthened with one layer with (A7, B7) and without (A2, B2) the end anchoring U-shaped strips. The comparisons show that at low- er loads the distributions are quite similar, while when the load in- creases the strains in the central part of the beam are greater in presence of the anchoring devices that therefore improve the stress transfer along the entire external reinforcement. 3.3. Cracking behavior In RC elements externally strengthened with FRP plates crack width is in general related to the value of steel stress and to the number of cracks formed (Ceroni et al. [31]). For the tested beams the comparison of results at the same value of steel stress, ﬁxed lower than yielding (i.e. 400 MPa that is about 90% of the yielding stress), has evidenced that as more cracks form as crack width de- creases; this reduction depends on the amount of external rein- forcement applied that, clearly, inﬂuences also the total load applied. In particular, the strengthened beams type B showed a reduc- tion of the experimental crack width compared to the reference beam higher (about 40%) than the beams type A (about 10%) with an higher number of cracks developed, even if the values of crack width and spacing of the corresponding reference beams (A1, B1) were comparable. Also for the beams strengthened with NSM carbon bars the crack width depends on the steel stress and the number of cracks; moreover the beams type A (A9 and A10) showed lower crack width and a smaller crack spacing compared to the beam A3 hav- ing a similar equivalent EBR reinforcement percentage. Finally as the steel stress is higher, for a given number of cracks, the crack width is larger. More details about the cracking behaviour of these beams and comparisons with some code provisions are reported in (Ceroni and Pecce [12]). 4. Experimental results of cyclic tests In order to deﬁne the cyclic loading history without inversion of sign for the beams A4 and B4 strengthened with two layers of CFRP, the load–deﬂection curves of the corresponding reference beams tested monotonically (A3 and B3) have been considered and some load levels have been ﬁxed. In particular, a service load, F serv, has been considered by dividing by 1.5 the experimental maximum load, Fmax, and two intermediate loads, F1 and F2, between Fserv and Fmax have been also ﬁxed, as is indicated in Fig. 8 a. The beams A4 and B4 were loaded according to the following protocol: – monotonic loading until Fserv, – 10 cycles between 0.8 Fserv and Fserv, – 10 cycles between Fserv and 0, – 10 cycles between F1 and 0, – 10 cycles between F2 and 0, – monotonic loading until failure. Because of the occurring of the end debonding of the FRP rein- forcement, the last 10 cycles at load F2 were not performed in both beams. The failure loads, Fmax, the values of deﬂection, dmax, and the tensile stress in the reinforcement at Fmax, and the load variation, DFexp, compared to the monotonic beams are reported in Table 5 . For the beam A4 a reduction of 10% against the equal monotonic beam A3 was observed with the same failure mode; for the beam B4 the reduction with respect to the specimen B3 was about 13%, conﬁrming a cyclic degradation of the bond behaviour at the FRP–concrete interface. Moreover the experimental tensile stresses in the FRP reinforcement at the mid-span was lower (about 780 MPa) than the values measured in the corresponding mono- tonic beams. Fig. 8. (a) Deﬁnition of load levels for cyclic load history without inversion

The load–displacement curves of the beams A4 (Fig. 9 a) and B4 (Fig. 9 b) show the increment in deformability and the residual deﬂection during cycles compared to the benchmark monotonic beams (A3 and B3); in particular for the beam B4, the increase in deformability and the residual deﬂection during cyclic loads was more signiﬁcant. Furthermore it can be observed that the mono- tonic curve is a good envelope of the cyclic ones. The beams A6, B6 and B6b strengthened on both sides with one layer of CFRP were subjected to cyclic load histories with inversion of sign (Fig. 8 b); due to the not symmetrical internal steel rein- forcement ( Fig. 1 a), the expected maximum positive and negative loads are different ( Fmax+ and /C0 Fmax/C0 ). In particular under the po- sitive loading condition, the service load, Fserv+, and the intermedi- ate loads, F1+ and F2+, are referred to the maximum load of the reference beams A2 and B2, according to the scheme of Fig. 8 a. On the contrary under the negative loading condition, the values /C0 Fserv/C0 , /C0 F1/C0 , /C0 F2/C0 , are referred to the maximum load of the beam A8 previously tested with the two steel bar diameter 8 mm in ten- sion. Basing on these assumptions, the planned load history was: – monotonic loading until F serv+, – 10 cycles between 0.8 Fserv+ and /C0 0.8 Fserv/C0 , – 10 cycles between Fserv+ and /C0 Fserv/C0 , – 10 cycles between F1+ and /C0 F1/C0 , – 10 cycles between F2+ and /C0 F2/C0 , – monotonic loading until failure. The negative loads were applied according to the same scheme of positive ones (Fig. 8 b) thanks to two steel plates connected by four steel bars that allow to invert the load sign (Fig. 8 c). In all beams failure was due to the end debonding of the exter- nal strengthening on the side having the lowest steel percentage (two bars /8). Moreover the failure loads were negative and very similar to the value attained by the monotonic beam A8 (27.8 kN) that is the reference one assumed to calculate the load variation (see Table 5 ). In Fig. 10 a the load–deﬂection relation of the beam A6 is pre- sented together with the curves of the beams A2 and A8 which are the two reference monotonic tests under positive and negative loads, respectively. In Fig. 10b the load–deﬂection relation is displayed for the beam B6b and for the corresponding monotonic beam B2 tested under positive loads: the reduction of stiffness and the increase of resid- ual deﬂection are greater under negative loading. In general, also for these beams it can be observed that the monotonic load–deﬂec- tion curve represents a good envelope of the cyclic ones. In Fig. 11a the strain–load relationships measured by the strain gauges placed at the mid-span are reported for the companion monotonic and cyclic beams B3 and B4; it can be observed that as the cycles grow the strains increase and are lower than the monotonic envelope. Analogously in Fig. 11 b, the behaviour of the strain gauge applied at the mid-span on the bottom side of the beams B2 and B6b is illustrated: in this case the FRP strength- ening behaves not similarly in tension and compression because an increasing of stiffness is noted after steel yielding under negative Table 5 Experimental results of beams tested under cyclic load history.
